elements.sent-2411-001: Differential privacy (DP) has recently been introduced into episodic reinforcement learning (RL) to formally address user privacy concerns in personalized services.
elements.sent-2411-002: Previous work mainly focuses on two trust models of DP: the central model, where a central agent is responsible for protecting users' sensitive data, and the (stronger) local model, where the protection occurs directly on the user side.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, they either require a trusted central agent or incur a significantly higher privacy cost, making it unsuitable for many scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-004: This work introduces a trust model stronger than the central model but with a lower privacy cost than the local model, leveraging the emerging shuffle model of privacy.
elements.sent-2411-005: We present the first generic algorithm for episodic RL under the shuffle model, where a trusted shuffler randomly permutes a batch of users' data before sending it to the central agent.
elements.sent-2411-006: We then instantiate the algorithm using our proposed shuffle Privatizer, relying on a shuffle private binary summation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our analysis shows that the algorithm achieves a near-optimal regret bound comparable to that of the centralized model and significantly outperforms the local model in terms of privacy cost.
elements.sent-2411-008: Reinforcement learning is a prominent sequential decision-making framework that has gained remarkable attraction in real-world applications across several fields such as healthcare \cite{gottesman2019guidelines , online recommendation \cite{afsar2022reinforcement , and language models \cite{ouyang2022training .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: In these applications, the learning agent continuously improves its performance by learning from users' personal feedback, which usually contains sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-010: Without privacy protection mechanisms in place, the learning agent can memorize information about users' interaction history \cite{carlini2019secret , which makes the learning agent vulnerable to various privacy attacks \cite{lei2023new .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Over the past decade, differential privacy \cite{dwork2014algorithmic has been extensively applied in various decision-making settings including multi-armed bandits \cite{tossou2016algorithms , linear control systems \cite{yazdani2022differentially , and network systems \cite{wang2017differential .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In the case of RL, there is a growing literature dealing with privacy issue of the reward function \cite{benvenuti2024differentially , the environment transition \cite{gohari2020privacy , and policies \cite{rajabi2022privacy .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Regarding privacy issues in interaction history, prior work focused on episodic RL in the regret setting under Joint DP (JDP) \cite{vietri2020private,baidifferentially and Local DP (LDP) constraints \cite{garcelon2021local,chowdhury2022differentially .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In these settings, each episode is regarded as an interaction with one user, and the aim is to ensure that the user's states and generated rewards will not be inferred by an adversary during the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-015: Specifically speaking, JDP guarantees that all other users' decisions will not leak much information about any specific user; it is known that $\pripara$-JDP can be obtained at the expense of an additive logarithmic term in the regret bound \cite{qiao2023near , $\cO(\sqrt{\episodetotal + \log(\episodetotal)/\pripara)$ after $K$ episodes.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, a trusted central agent is required to collect the raw interaction history under JDP, which may render it infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-017: On the other hand, $\pripara$-LDP provides a stronger privacy guarantee, where each user's raw data is privatized on their local side before being sent to the learning agent.
elements.sent-2411-018: But it leads to a significantly worse regret, $\cO(\sqrt{\episodetotal /\pripara)$ after $K$ episodes \cite{garcelon2021local , which could be unsatisfactory in high privacy regimes, i.e., when $\pripara$ is chosen small.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: This naturally leads to the following question: Can a finer trade-off between privacy and regret in RL be achieved, i.e., achieving utility comparable to that of centralized privacy models but without relying on a trusted central agent? Motivated by these, this paper focuses on the online RL problem under an intermediate trust model of differential privacy, known as the shuffle differential privacy (SDP) \cite{cheu2019distributed in the hope of attaining a finer regret-privacy trade-off.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this new trust model, a secure shuffler is assumed between the users and the central agent, which is often implemented by a trusted third party via cryptographic mixnets or trusted hardware \cite{bittau2017prochlo .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The shuffler permutes a batch of users' noisy data before they are viewed by the agent so that it can not distinguish two users' data.
elements.sent-2411-022: The shuffle model provides a stronger privacy guarantee than the central model but usually suffers a smaller cost than the local model, which has achieved a good privacy/utility trade-off in several learning problems such as (federated) supervised learning \cite{lowy2023private , and bandits \cite{tenenbaum2021differentially,chowdhury2022shuffle .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, it is still not investigated within RL to our best knowledge, due to challenges that arise upon applying algorithmic ideas from simpler decision-making models like bandits.
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, the SDP model relies on a batch updating mechanism and data perturbation, which inherently delays exploitation and complicates exploration.
elements.sent-2411-025: This creates many difficulties in the algorithm design regret analysis, making it challenging to develop an effective private RL algorithm that attains sublinear regret in $K$ while ensuring SDP constraints.

elements.sent-2411-029: However, the existing methods cannot be directly applied to our problem, since we must control the impact of the privacy perturbation on the learning process.

elements.sent-2411-034: Additionally, SDP-PE matches the best results in the centralized model regarding the dependence on $\episodetotal$ and $\pripara$ \cite{qiao2023near , achieving an improved privacy-regret trade-off.}

elements.sent-2411-043: An episodic Markov decision process (MDP) is defined by a tuple $\left(\statespace, \actionspace, \horizontotal, \{\transeasy_\horizon\}_{\horizon=1 ^\horizontotal, \{\rewdis_\horizon\}_{\horizon=1 ^\horizontotal, d_1 \right)$, where $\statespace,\actionspace$ are state and action spaces with respective cardinalities $\statesize$ and $\actionsize$, and $\horizontotal$ is the episode length.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: At step $\horizon$, the transition function $\transeasy_\horizon(\cdot\vert\state,\action)$ takes a state-action pair and returns a distribution over states, the reward distribution $\rewdis_\horizon(\state,\action)$ is a distribution over $\{0,1\}$ with expectation $\reward_\horizon(\state,\action)$, and $d_1$ is the distribution of initial state.\footnote{For simplicity, we assume that the rewards are binary.}
elements.sent-2411-045: However, our results naturally extend to $[0,1]$, by replacing our private binary summation mechanism (defined in Section \ref{sec: Privacy Guarantees ) with a private summation mechanism for real numbers in $[0, 1]$ with similar guarantees.}
elements.sent-2411-046: A deterministic policy is defined as a collection $\policy=(\policy_1,\dots, \policy_\horizontotal)$ of policies $\policy_\horizon: \statespace\rightarrow\actionspace$.
elements.sent-2411-047: The value function $\valuef_\horizon^\policy$ and Q function $\qvaluef_\horizon^\policy$ are defined as: $\valuef_\horizon^\policy(\state) = \EE_\policy[\sum_{t=\horizon ^\horizontotal \reward_t\vert \state_\horizon=\state], \qvaluef_\horizon^\policy(\state,\action) = \EE_\policy[\sum_{t=\horizon ^\horizontotal \reward_t\vert \state_\horizon,\action_\horizon=\state,\action], \forall\state,\action\in\statespace\times\actionspace$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: There exists an optimal deterministic policy $\policy^\star$ such that $\valuef_\horizon^\star(\state) = \valuef_\horizon^{\policy^\star (\state) = \max_\policy \valuef_\horizon^\policy(\state)$ for all $\state,\horizon\in\statespace\times[\horizontotal]$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The Bellman (optimality) equation follows $\forall \horizon\in[\horizontotal]: \qvaluef_\horizon^\star(\state,\action) =\reward_\horizon(\state,\action) + \max_{\action'}\expect_{\state'\sim\transeasy_\horizon(\state,\action) [\valuef_{\horizon+1 ^{\star (\state')].$ The optimal policy is the greedy policy: $\policy^\star_\horizon(\state ) = \argmax_{\action \qvaluef_\horizon^\star(\state,\action),\forall \state\in\statespace.$ For generalization, we define the value function of $\policy$ under MDP transition $p$ and reward function $r'$ as $\valuef^\pi(r',p)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: We assume the learning agent (e.g., a personalized service) interacts with an unknown MDP for $\episodetotal$ episodes.
elements.sent-2411-051: Each episode $\episode\in[\episodetotal]$ is regarded as the interaction with a unique user $\user_\episode\in\userspace$, where $\userspace$ is the user space.
elements.sent-2411-052: Following \cite{vietri2020private , a user $\user_\episode$ can be seen as a tree of depth $\horizontotal$ encoding the state and reward responses they would reply to all $\actionsize^\horizontotal$ possible sequences of actions from the agent.}
elements.sent-2411-053: For each episode $\episode\in[\episodetotal]$, the learner determines policy $\policy_\episode$ and sends it to user $\user_\episode$ for execution.
elements.sent-2411-054: The output of the execution, a trajectory $\traj_\episode= \rbr{\state_\horizon^\episode,\action_\horizon^\episode,\reward_\horizon^\episode _{\horizon\in[\horizontotal]}$, is sent back to the learner for updating the policy.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We measure the performance of a learning algorithm by its cumulative regret after $\episodetotal$ episodes, \begin{equation}\regret := \sum_{\episode=1}^\episodetotal \sbr{ \valuef_1^\star(\state_1^\episode) - \valuef_1^{\policy_\episode}(\state_1^\episode)},\end{equation} where $\state_1^\episode$ is the initial state sampled from $d_1$.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Shuffle Differential Privacy for Episodic RL
elements.sent-2411-056: Consider a general case where $\trajset$ is the data universe, and we have $n$ unique users.
elements.sent-2411-057: We say $\batchdata, \batchdata' \in \trajset^n$ are neighboring batched datasets if they only differ in one user's data for some $i\in[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-058: Then, we have the standard definition of differential privacy \cite{dwork2014algorithmic :.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The special case of $(\pripara,0)$-DP is also called pure DP, whereas, for $\priconf>0$, $(\pripara,\priconf)$-DP is referred to as approximate DP.. Shuffle Differential Privacy.
elements.sent-2411-060: A standard shuffle-model protocol $\protocol = (\randomizer,\shuffler,\analyzer)$ consists of three parts: (i) a (local) random encoder $\randomizer$ at each user's side; (ii) a secure shuffler $\shuffler$; and (iii) an analyzer $\analyzer$ at the central server.
elements.sent-2411-061: For $n$ users, each user $\user_i$ first locally applies the encoder $\randomizer$ on its raw data $\batchdata_i$ and sends the resulting messages $\randomizer(\batchdata_i)$ to the shuffler $\shuffler$.
elements.sent-2411-062: The shuffler $\shuffler$ permutes messages from all the batch users uniformly at random and then reports $ \shuffler(\randomizer(\batchdata_1),\dots,\randomizer(\batchdata_n))$ to the analyzer.
elements.sent-2411-063: The analyzer $\analyzer$ then aggregates the received messages from the shuffler and outputs desired statistics.
elements.sent-2411-064: In this protocol, the users trust the shuffler but not the analyzer.
elements.sent-2411-065: Hence, the goal is to ensure the output of the shuffler $\shuffler$ on two neighboring datasets is indistinguishable in the analyzer's view.
elements.sent-2411-066: Here, we define the mechanism $(\shuffler\circ\randomizer^n)(\batchdata) = \shuffler(\randomizer(\batchdata_1),\dots,\randomizer(\batchdata_n))$, where $\batchdata\in\batchdataspace^n$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Finally, we have the definition of shuffle DP \cite{cheu2019distributed :.}
elements.sent-2411-069: To adapt the shuffle model into RL protocol, it is natural to divide total $\episodetotal$ users into $\batchnumber$ batches as in \cite{tenenbaum2021differentially , with each user's data being their trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Let $\batchlength_\batchindx$ denote the size of batch $\batchindx$, such that $\episodetotal=\sum_{\batchindx=1 ^\batchnumber \batchlength_\batchindx$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: For each batch $\batchindx\in[\batchnumber]$, $\batchlength_\batchindx$ users execute the same policy and generate a batch dataset containing their trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-072: The shuffle-model protocol is then applied to this dataset to output desired private statistics, which are subsequently sent to the learner for policy updates.
elements.sent-2411-073: Here, instead of a single-batch output, one needs to protect the outputs across all $\batchnumber$ batches.
elements.sent-2411-074: To this end, we define the (composite) mechanism $\cM_\protocol = (\shuffler\circ\randomizer^{\batchlength_1 ,\dots,\shuffler\circ\randomizer^{\batchlength_\batchnumber ),$ where each mechanism $\shuffler\circ\randomizer^{\batchlength_\batchindx}$ operates on a trajectories dataset of $\batchlength_\batchindx$ users.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: With this notation, we have the following definition \cite{cheu2019distributed .}
elements.sent-2411-076: In the central DP model \cite{vietri2020private , the privacy burden lies with a central server, which injects carefully designed noise into the necessary statistics to protect privacy.}
elements.sent-2411-077: On the other hand, in the local DP model \cite{garcelon2021local , each user’s data is privatized by adding noise to their local data.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In contrast, in the shuffle privacy model, privacy without a trusted central server is achieved by ensuring that the inputs to the analyzer $\analyzer$ already satisfy DP.
elements.sent-2411-079: Specifically, by incorporating a secure shuffler $\shuffler$ and properly adjusting the noise level in the encoder $\randomizer$, we ensure that the final added noise in the aggregated data over the batch of users matches the noise that would have otherwise been added by the central server in the central model.
elements.sent-2411-080: Through this, the shuffle model provides the possibility to achieve similar utility to the central model, but maintain privacy without a trusted central server.
elements.sent-2411-081: In this section, we introduce a generic algorithmic framework, Shuffle Differentially Private Policy Elimination (SDP-PE, Algorithm \ref{algo: Private Batch-based Policy Elimination ).}
elements.sent-2411-082: Unlike existing private RL algorithms that rely on the optimism in the face of uncertainty principle \cite{vietri2020private,garcelon2021local , SDP-PE builds on the policy elimination idea from \cite{qiao2022sample , which extends action elimination from bandits to RL.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: At a high level, SDP-PE divides the exploration process into stages, with exponentially increasing batch sizes.
elements.sent-2411-084: In each stage, it maintains an active policy set $\policyset$, and refines value estimates and confidence intervals for all active policies using the private model estimate from sufficient shuffle-private statistics.
elements.sent-2411-085: These value estimates are then used to eliminate policies that are likely sub-optimal.
elements.sent-2411-086: At the last stage, the remaining policies are guaranteed to be near-optimal.
elements.sent-2411-087: An important aspect of SDP-PE is the concept of forgetting, where only the data of the current stage is used to estimate the model, preventing noise accumulation from earlier stages.
elements.sent-2411-088: To estimate the value for all active policies with uniform convergence, we address this problem by obtaining an accurate estimate of the transition and reward functions.
elements.sent-2411-089: This requires sufficient exploration for each state-action pair, and injecting appropriate noise into the visitation counts and cumulative rewards of these pairs to preserve privacy.
elements.sent-2411-090: However, some states are rarely visited due to their very low transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-091: To remedy this, we construct an absorbing MDP that replaces these infrequent-visited states with an absorbing state $\absorbstate$.
elements.sent-2411-092: For the remaining states, we ensure they are visited sufficiently often by some policy in $\policyset$, allowing the estimated value to uniformly approximate the true value under the original MDP.
elements.sent-2411-093: Inspired by the APEVE algorithm in \cite{qiao2022sample , we design SDP-PE shown in Algorithm \ref{algo: Private Batch-based Policy Elimination .}}
elements.sent-2411-094: It divides the $\episodetotal$ episodes (users) into a sequence of stages.
elements.sent-2411-095: Each stage $\batchindx$ consists of three steps, and the batch length for sub-steps grows exponentially as $\batchlength_\batchindx:=2^\batchindx$ for $\batchindx=1,\dots,\batchnumber$, with $\batchnumber = O(\log\episodetotal)$.
elements.sent-2411-096: Crude Exploration: For each pair ($\state,\action$), explore them layer by layer from scratch using the policy in the current policy set $\policyset_\batchindx$ that has the highest visitation probability.
elements.sent-2411-097: We apply shuffle Privatizer to the exploration data of each layer to obtain the private counts.
elements.sent-2411-098: These counts help identify the infrequently-visited tuples $\infreqtuples$, and construct a private crude estimate $\crutrans$ of the corresponding absorbing MDP $\abtrans$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Fine Exploration: Using the crude transition estimate from the previous step, we explore each $\horizon,\state,\action$ with the policy that has the highest visitation probability under the crude transition model.
elements.sent-2411-100: We then apply the shuffle Privatizer to the entire exploration data from this step and construct a refined private estimate $\reftrans$ of $\abtrans$ and reward estimate $\rewprieasy^\batchindx$.
elements.sent-2411-101: Policy Elimination: We evaluate all policies in $\policyset_\batchindx$ using the refined estimates $\reftrans$ and $\rewprieasy^\batchindx$.
elements.sent-2411-102: The active policy set is updated by eliminating all policies whose value upper confidence bound (UCB) is less than the lower confidence bound (LCB) of any other active policy.
elements.sent-2411-103: As the algorithm proceeds and more data is collected in further stages, our confidence intervals shrink, along with the policy elimination step, ensuring that the optimal policy stays in the active policy set with high probability, which guarantees that the algorithm eventually converges to the optimal policy.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Shuffle Private Policy Elimination} Parameters: Episode number $\episodetotal$, universal constant $C$, failure probability $\delta$, privacy budget $\pripara > 0$ and a Privatizer. \newline Initialization: \sloppy $\policyset_1 = \{\text{all the deterministic policies}\}$, $\iota = \log(2\horizontotal\actionsize\episodetotal/\delta)$. \text{Set precision levels $\confcountxa$ for Privatizer.} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$\batchindx=1$ to $\batchnumber$} \IF{$2(\sum_{i=1}^\batchindx \batchlength_i)\geq\episodetotal$} \STATE $\batchlength_\batchindx = \frac{\episodetotal - 2(\sum_{i=1}^\batchindx \batchlength_i)}{2}$. (o.w. $\batchlength_\batchindx = 2^\batchindx$) \ENDIF \STATE $\infreqtuples^\batchindx,\crutrans = $ Crude Exploration($\policyset_\batchindx,\batchlength_\batchindx,\pripara$,Privatizer). \STATE $\reftrans,\rewprieasy^\batchindx =$ Fine Exploration($\infreqtuples^\batchindx,\crutrans,\policyset_\batchindx,\batchlength_\batchindx,\pripara$, Privatizer). \STATE $\elimpolicyset_\batchindx = \emptyset$ \FOR{$\policy \in \policyset_\batchindx$} \IF{$\sup_{\policy'\in\policyset_\batchindx}\valuef^{\policy'}(\rewprieasy^\batchindx,\reftrans) - \valuef^\policy(\rewprieasy^\batchindx,\reftrans) \geq 2C\big(\sqrt{\frac{\horizontotal^5\statesize^2\actionsize\iota}{\batchlength_\batchindx}}+\frac{\statesize^3\actionsize^2\horizontotal^5\confcountxa\iota}{\batchlength_\batchindx}\big)$} \STATE Update $\elimpolicyset_\batchindx\leftarrow \elimpolicyset_\batchindx \cup \{\policy\}$. \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE $\policyset_{\batchindx+1} \leftarrow \policyset_{\batchindx} \backslash \elimpolicyset_\batchindx$. \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic} \label{algo: Private Batch-based Policy Elimination}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Counts in Algorithm \ref{algo: Private Batch-based Policy Elimination}

elements.subsection-2411-004: Crude Exploration in Algorithm \ref{algo: Crude Exploration}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Crude Exploration} Input: Policy set $\policyset$, number of episodes $\batchlength$, privacy budget $\pripara$ and a Privatizer. Initialization: $\batchlength_0 = \frac{\batchlength}{\horizontotal\statesize\actionsize}, C_1=6, \infreqtuples=\emptyset,\iota = \log(2\horizontotal\actionsize\episodetotal/\delta)$. $1_{\horizon,\state,\action}$ is a reward function $\reward'$ where $\reward'_{\horizon'}(\state',\action') = \ind\{(\horizon',\state',\action')=(\horizon,\state,\action)\}$. $\absorbstate$ is an additional absorbing state. $\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}}$ is a transition function over extended space $\statespace\cup\{\absorbstate\}\times\actionspace$, initialized arbitrarily. Output: Infrequent tuples $\infreqtuples$, and crude estimated transition function $\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}}$ \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$\horizon=1$ to $\horizontotal$} \STATE Set data set $\batchdata^\horizon = \emptyset$. \FOR{$(\state,\action)\in\statespace\times\actionspace$} \STATE $\policy_{\horizon,\state,\action} = \argmax_{\policy\in\policyset} \valuef^\policy(1_{\horizon,\state,\action},\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}})$ \STATE Run $\policy_{\horizon,\state,\action}$ for $\batchlength_0$ episodes, and add the trajectories into dataset $\batchdata^{\horizon}$. \ENDFOR \STATE Send batch dataset $\batchdata^{\horizon}$ to the Privatizer. \STATE Receive private counts $\visitxatotalprieasy_\horizon(\state,\action,\state')$ and $\visitxaxtotalprieasy_\horizon(\state,\action)$ for all $(\state,\action,\state')$ in $\horizon$-th horizon from Privatizer. \STATE $\infreqtuples = \infreqtuples\cup\{(\horizon,\state,\action,\state')\vert \visitxaxtotalprieasy_\horizon(\state,\action,\state') \leq C_1\confcountxa\horizontotal^2\iota\}$ \STATE $\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}} = \text{Estimate Transition}(\visitxatotalprieasy_\horizon,\infreqtuples,\absorbstate,\horizon,\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}}).$ \ENDFOR \RETURN $\{\infreqtuples,\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}}\}$. \end{algorithmic} \label{algo: Crude Exploration}
elements.sent-2411-113: To learn an accurate private estimate of $\transeasy_\horizon(\state'\vert\action,\state)$ for any tuple $(\horizon,\state,\action,\state')$, it is necessary to collect private counts that occur at least $O(\confcountxa\horizontotal^2\iota)$ times for each tuple.
elements.sent-2411-114: Thus, we try to visit each tuple as frequently as possible by using policies from active policy set $\policyset$.
elements.sent-2411-115: We define the set of infrequently visited tuples $(\horizon,\state,\action,\state')$ as $\infreqtuples$, which consists of all the tuples $(\horizon,\state,\action,\state')$ that are visited less than $O(\confcountxa\horizontotal^2\iota)$ times in current exploration.
elements.sent-2411-116: For the tuples not in $\infreqtuples$, we can get accurate estimates, and for tuples in $\infreqtuples$, they have little influence on the value estimate.
elements.sent-2411-117: In crude exploration step, we perform layer-wise exploration.
elements.sent-2411-118: During the exploration of the $\horizon$-th layer, we construct $\policy_{\horizon,\state,\action}$ that has the highest visit probability for $(\horizon,\state,\action)$ under the private crude estimate $\crutrans$, and then run each $\policy_{\horizon,\state,\action}$ for the same number of episodes.
elements.sent-2411-119: At the same time, we collect the interaction history for the $\horizon$-th layer as a batch dataset $\batchdata^\horizon$, and send it to the Privatizer to generate the private counts of this batch.
elements.sent-2411-120: The private counts are then used to update the infrequent tuple set $\infreqtuples$ and crude estimate of the $\horizon$-th layer.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Fine Exploration} Input: Infrequent tuples $\infreqtuples$, crude estimated transition $\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}}$, policy set $\policyset$, number of episodes $\batchlength$, privacy budget $\pripara$ and a Privatizer. Initialization: $\batchlength_0 = \frac{\batchlength}{\horizontotal\statesize\actionsize}, \batchdata=\emptyset$. $1_{\horizon,\state,\action}$ is a reward function $\reward'$ where $\reward'_{\horizon'}(\state',\action') = \ind\{(\horizon',\state',\action')=(\horizon,\state,\action)\}$. Initialize refined transition estimate $\tilde{P}^{\text{ref}} = \tilde{P}^{\text{cru}}$. elements.sent-2411-369: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a powerful tool for sequential decision-making, but its application is often hindered by privacy concerns arising from its interaction data.
elements.sent-2411-370: This challenge is particularly acute in advanced Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), where learning from operational and user data can expose systems to privacy inference attacks.
elements.sent-2411-371: Existing differential privacy (DP) models for RL are often inadequate: the centralized model requires a fully trusted server, creating a single point of failure risk, while the local model incurs significant performance degradation that is unsuitable for many control applications.
elements.sent-2411-372: This paper addresses this gap by leveraging the emerging shuffle model of privacy, an intermediate trust model that provides strong privacy guarantees without a centralized trust assumption.
elements.sent-2411-373: We present Shuffle Differentially Private Policy Elimination (SDP-PE), the first generic policy elimination-based algorithm for episodic RL under the shuffle model.
elements.sent-2411-374: Our method introduces a novel exponential batching schedule and a ``forgetting'' mechanism to balance the competing demands of privacy and learning performance.
elements.sent-2411-375: Our analysis shows that SDP-PE achieves a near-optimal regret bound, demonstrating a superior privacy-regret trade-off that significantly outperforms the local model.
elements.sent-2411-376: This work establishes the viability of the shuffle model for secure data-driven control in advanced CPS.
elements.sent-2411-377: In these systems, a learning agent continuously interacts with the environment to receive new observations and rewards, and improves the performance of its policy.
elements.sent-2411-378: However, a crucial concern for its deployment is that the interaction data usually contains sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-379: This concern is not only limited to personal data in user-centric CPS~\cite{9411689 , but also extends to confidential operational data, such as control parameters or system states in a distributed network~\cite{9573387 .}}
elements.sent-2411-380: Without privacy protection mechanisms in place, the learning agent can memorize sensitive information from the interaction history~\cite{zhang2016privacy , exposing confidential operational patterns or leaving the system's underlying entities vulnerable to various privacy attack~\cite{mo2024security .}}
elements.sent-2411-381: To counter such threat, differential privacy (DP)~\cite{dwork2014algorithmic has been extensively applied as a formal privacy method in various decision-making settings, including multi-armed bandits~\cite{wang2025privatebandittdsc , classical control systems~\cite{10750420,10426767 , and networked control systems~\cite{10772998,9954449 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-382: In the case of RL, a growing body of literature has addressed the privacy of different components of the learning process, such as protecting the reward function~\cite{benvenuti2024differentially , the transition dynamics~\cite{gohari2020privacy , or the learned policies~\cite{gan2023differentially .}}}
elements.sent-2411-383: Distinct from this line of work, our focus is on protecting the interaction history itself.
elements.sent-2411-384: Prior work has primarily focused on two privacy architectures: the centralized privacy model with Joint DP (JDP) constraint~\cite{vietri2020private,baidifferentially and the local privacy model with Local DP (LDP) constraint~\cite{garcelon2021local,chowdhury2022differentially .}}
elements.sent-2411-385: To apply these frameworks, the standard approach is to consider an episodic online interaction setting.
elements.sent-2411-386: In this formulation, the agent interacts with the environment for $\episodetotal$ episodes, and each episode is regarded as an interaction with one specific user.
elements.sent-2411-387: In a CPS context, this ``user'' could be a human customer, a smart home, or an autonomous vehicle providing data.
elements.sent-2411-388: The aim is to ensure that the user's states and generated rewards will not be inferred by an adversary during the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-389: Specifically, JDP guarantees that all other users' decisions will not leak much information about any specific user, and $\pripara$-JDP can be obtained at the expense of an additive logarithmic term of $\cO(\log(\episodetotal)/\pripara)$ in the regret bound~\cite{vietri2020private .}
elements.sent-2411-390: However, a trusted central server is required to collect and process the raw interaction data under JDP, which may result in a single point of failure.
elements.sent-2411-391: On the other hand, $\pripara$-LDP removes this risk by privatizing data on each users' local device, but at the cost of severe performance degradation, resulting in a multiplicative regret term $\cO(\sqrt{\episodetotal /\pripara)$~\cite{garcelon2021local that is often unacceptable for control applications.}}
elements.sent-2411-392: This naturally leads to the following question: Can a finer trade-off between privacy and regret in RL be achieved, i.e., achieving utility comparable to that of centralized privacy models but without relying on a trusted central server?.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{./figs/privacy-model-0811.pdf} \caption{Illustration of the operational workflows in the centralized, local, and shuffle privacy models.} \label{fig: privacy-model}
elements.sent-2411-393: Motivated by these, this paper leverages an intermediate trust model of differential privacy, known as shuffle differential privacy (SDP)~\cite{cheu2019distributed , as a resilient, distributed architecture for private RL.}
elements.sent-2411-394: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig: privacy-model , this paradigm uses a secure shuffler to anonymize a batch of locally randomized data before it reaches the central server, and makes sure that the central server can not distinguish any two users' data.}
elements.sent-2411-395: The shuffler is trusted by these users since it can be implemented by trusted cryptographic mixnets or trusted hardware deployed via distributed constructs~\cite{bittau2017prochlo .}
elements.sent-2411-396: This privacy model provides a stronger privacy guarantee than the centralized model but usually suffers a smaller cost than the local model.
elements.sent-2411-397: The key intuition is that additional randomness of the shuffler creates a privacy blanket~\cite{balle2019privacy so that each user can inject much less noise and hide their information in the crowd.}
elements.sent-2411-398: A better privacy-utility trade-off has been proven in related fields like (federated) supervised learning~\cite{lowy2023private,lowy2023private2 , and bandits~\cite{tenenbaum2021differentially,hanna2024differentially .}}
elements.sent-2411-399: However, to the best of our knowledge, the application of the shuffle model in RL remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-400: This gap is due to a fundamental tension between the batch-processing requirement of shuffle privacy and the sequential, online nature of most RL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-401: Specifically, the shuffle model introduces two compounding challenges.
elements.sent-2411-402: First, by operating on anonymized batches, it imposes an inherent delay, as the agent receives no feedback until an entire batch of interactions is complete.
elements.sent-2411-403: Second, to ensure privacy, the aggregated data is intentionally perturbed with statistical noise.
elements.sent-2411-404: This combination of delayed feedback and noisy observations severely complicates the crucial exploration-exploitation trade-off, posing a significant challenge to developing private RL algorithms that can achieve near-optimal regret while satisfying the SDP constraint.
elements.sent-2411-405: The batch-based updates required by SDP makes the literature on multi-batch RL highly relevant.
elements.sent-2411-406: This field aims to improve deployment efficiency by minimizing the number of policy switches while maintaining low regret.
elements.sent-2411-407: Approaches in this area are typically either adaptive~\cite{bai2019provably,johnson2024sampleefficiency , where batch sizes are determined adaptively based on online data collection, or static~\cite{qiao2022sample,zhang2022near , where the batch schedule is fixed in advance and offer no intra-batch adaptivity.}}
elements.sent-2411-408: This line of work has produced algorithms that limit the number of policy switches to an optimal $\cO(\log\log\episodetotal)$, while maintaining an optimal $\tilde{\cO (\sqrt{\episodetotal )$ regret.}}
elements.sent-2411-409: The static approach is more relevant to our context, as its core constraint, not using data until a batch is complete, naturally aligns with the shuffle privacy protocol.
elements.sent-2411-410: However, these existing methods are not directly applicable since they can access accurate interaction histories to optimize the policy.
elements.sent-2411-411: In contrast, our algorithm must be robust to the significant noise injected to ensure privacy.
elements.sent-2411-412: This requires a fundamentally different design, one where the batching mechanism and behavior policies are carefully co-designed to control and mitigate the impact of these privacy-induced perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-413: In this paper, we overcome these challenges by introducing Shuffle Differentially Private Policy Elimination (SDP-PE), the first RL algorithm designed to satisfy the constraint of shuffle privacy.
elements.sent-2411-414: We provide both rigorous theoretical analysis and empirical validation for our method, and the main contributions are threefold:.
elements.sent-2411-415: Generic Algorithm Design: Based on the policy elimination idea~\cite{qiao2022sample , we develop two key strategies tailored for the shuffle model: a) a staged learning process with exponentially increasing batch sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-416: This structure allows for the iterative refinement of an active policy set, and is critical for managing the exploration-exploitation trade-off under the delayed feedback inherent to the shuffle model.
elements.sent-2411-417: This approach prevents the accumulation of privacy-induced noise across the learning stages.
elements.sent-2411-418: Novel SDP Privatizer for RL: We design a new SDP Privatizer that provides strong utility guarantees for the statistical estimates required in RL.
elements.sent-2411-419: Built upon a shuffle-private binary summation protocol, our privatizer is designed for seamless integration into the SDP-PE framework, ensuring both rigorous privacy and high performance.
elements.sent-2411-420: Theoretical Results: We prove that SDP-PE obtains a regret of $\widetilde{\cO}\big(\sqrt{\statesize\actionsize\horizontotal^3\episodetotal + \frac{\statesize^3\actionsize\horizontotal^6 {\pripara}\big)$,\footnote{Here $\tilde{\mathcal{O (\cdot)$ hides terms that are poly-logarithmic in $\episodetotal$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-421: This result matches the information-theoretic lower bound in standard episodic RL up to lower-order terms, demonstrating a superior privacy-performance trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-422: These findings are further corroborated by our numerical experiments.
elements.sent-2411-423: We summarize the comparison of best-known results under three distinct privacy guarantees in Table~\ref{table: contributions : $\pripara$-JDP, $\pripara$-LDP, and $(\pripara,\priconf)$-SDP, where $\pripara$ and $\priconf$ refer to the privacy budget and probability of privacy failure, respectively.}

elements.sent-2411-430: In this section, we review the necessary background on episodic reinforcement learning and shuffle differential privacy, and then formally state the private RL problem.
elements.sent-2411-431: There exists an optimal policy $\policy^\star$ such that $\valuef_\horizon^\star(\state) = \valuef_\horizon^{\policy^\star (\state) = \max_\policy \valuef_\horizon^\policy(\state)$ for all $\state,\horizon\in\statespace\times[\horizontotal]$.\footnote{For a positive integer $n$, we define $[n]\!:=\!\{1,\ldots,n\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-432: We consider an agent (e.g., a controller) tasked with learning to interact with unknown dynamics, modeled as an MDP.
elements.sent-2411-433: The agent interacts with the environment for $\episodetotal$ episodes.
elements.sent-2411-434: In the security-conscious CPS setting, each episode $\episode\in[\episodetotal]$ is modeled as an interaction with a unique system component or user $\user_\episode\in\userspace$, where $\userspace$ is the user space.
elements.sent-2411-435: For each episode $\episode\in[\episodetotal]$, the learning agent determines policy $\policy_\episode$ and sends it to user $\user_\episode$ for execution.
elements.sent-2411-436: The output of the execution, a trajectory $\traj_\episode= \rbr{\state_\horizon^\episode,\action_\horizon^\episode,\reward_\horizon^\episode _{\horizon\in[\horizontotal]}$ representing the sensitive data, is sent back to the learning agent for updating the policy.}
elements.sent-2411-437: Differential Privacy provides a formal guarantee of data privacy.
elements.sent-2411-438: Then, we have the standard definition of DP~\cite{dwork2014algorithmic :.}
elements.sent-2411-439: Here, the randomized mechanism $\cM$ is an algorithm that takes a dataset as input and uses internal randomness to produce a privatized output, such as a statistic.
elements.sent-2411-440: The special case of $(\pripara,0)$-DP is also called pure DP, whereas, for $\priconf>0$, $(\pripara,\priconf)$-DP is referred to as approximate DP.
elements.sent-2411-441: The shuffle model is an enhanced DP protocol $\protocol = (\randomizer,\shuffler,\analyzer)$ involving three parts, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig: privacy-model : (i) a (local) random encoder $\randomizer$ at each user's side; (ii) a secure shuffler $\shuffler$; and (iii) an analyzer $\analyzer$ at the central server (i.e., the learning agent).}
elements.sent-2411-442: In this model, each user $\user_i$ first locally randomizes their data $\batchdata_i$ via the encoder $\randomizer$, and sends the resulting messages $\randomizer(\batchdata_i)$ to the shuffler $\shuffler$.
elements.sent-2411-443: The shuffler $\shuffler$ permutes messages from this batch users at random and then reports $ \shuffler(\randomizer(\batchdata_1),\dots,\randomizer(\batchdata_n))$ to the analyzer.
elements.sent-2411-444: The analyzer $\analyzer$ then aggregates the received messages and outputs desired statistics.
elements.sent-2411-445: In this protocol, the users trust the shuffler but not the analyzer, and the goal is to ensure the output of the shuffler $\shuffler$ on two neighboring datasets is indistinguishable in the analyzer's view.
elements.sent-2411-446: Thus, we have the definition of standard shuffle DP~\cite{cheu2019distributed :.}
elements.sent-2411-447: In a sequential data-generation setting, this protocol will be applied across multiple batches.
elements.sent-2411-448: To protect the sequence of outputs across all $\batchnumber$ batches, we define the (composite) mechanism $\cM_\protocol = (\shuffler\circ\randomizer^{\batchlength_1 ,\dots,\shuffler\circ\randomizer^{\batchlength_\batchnumber ),$ where each mechanism $\shuffler\circ\randomizer^{\batchlength_\batchindx}$ operates on a dataset of $\batchlength_\batchindx$ users.}}
elements.sent-2411-449: This leads to the following definition $\batchnumber$-batch SDP \cite{cheu2019distributed :.}
elements.sent-2411-450: Here, we formally define the private reinforcement learning problem.
elements.sent-2411-451: We consider an agent interacting with an unknown episodic MDP as described in Section~\ref{subsec: episodic rl , but under the communication constraint of the $\batchnumber$-batch shuffle privacy defined in Section~\ref{subsec: dp and shuffle model .}}
elements.sent-2411-452: The core of our problem lies in the information feedback loop.
elements.sent-2411-453: The learning process is partitioned into $\batchnumber$ batches.
elements.sent-2411-454: In each batch $\batchindx$, the agent deploys some policy to a group of $\batchlength_\batchindx$ users, and each user's resulting interaction trajectory is considered their private data.
elements.sent-2411-455: Crucially, the agent does not observe the raw interaction trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-456: Instead, they are processed by a shuffle protocol, and the agent only receives a privatized, aggregated output for that batch, which it then uses to select policies for the subsequent batch.
elements.sent-2411-457: The central challenge is to design an algorithm that achieves a dual objective:.
elements.sent-2411-458: Learning performance: Minimize the cumulative regret $\regret$.
elements.sent-2411-459: System privacy: Guarantee that the entire sequence of outputs observed by the agent satisfies a $\batchnumber$-batch $(\pripara, \priconf)$-SDP guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-460: This formulation captures the fundamental tension of our work: the agent must learn to make effective sequential decisions despite receiving only delayed, batched, and noisy information.
elements.sent-2411-461: Unlike existing private RL algorithms that rely on the optimism in the face of uncertainty principle \cite{vietri2020private,garcelon2021local , SDP-PE builds on the policy elimination idea from \cite{qiao2022sample,zhang2022near , which extends action elimination from bandits~\cite{evendar06a to RL.}}}
elements.sent-2411-462: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig: SDP-PE , SDP-PE operates in a sequence of stages with exponentially increasing batch sizes, where $\batchlength_\batchindx:=2^\batchindx$ for $\batchindx=1,\dots,\batchnumber$ and $\batchnumber = \cO(\log\episodetotal)$.}
elements.sent-2411-463: Within each stage, the algorithm maintains a set of ``active" policies ($\policyset$) and follows a three-step procedure: Crude Exploration, Fine Exploration, and Policy Elimination.
elements.sent-2411-464: The process begins by broadly exploring the environment to build a preliminary model.
elements.sent-2411-465: This model then guides a more refined exploration phase to gather higher-quality data and keep low regret.
elements.sent-2411-466: Finally, using this refined data, it constructs private confidence bounds on the value of each active policy and eliminates any policy that is provably sub-optimal.
elements.sent-2411-467: This cycle of explore-refine-eliminate repeats across stages.
elements.sent-2411-468: As the algorithm proceeds and the batch sizes grow, the confidence intervals shrink, ensuring that only near-optimal policies remain.
elements.sent-2411-469: The high-level, three-step structure of our algorithm is inspired by the APEVE framework of \cite{qiao2022sample .}
elements.sent-2411-470: However, our objective of minimizing regret under shuffle privacy, rather than minimizing policy switches, necessitates several fundamental innovations in the design as detailed below.
elements.sent-2411-471: We adapt the technique of using an absorbing MDP from APEVE to handle rarely visited states.
elements.sent-2411-472: The key difference is that in our setting, the set of ``infrequently-visited" tuples must be identified from private counts (cf.~Assumption \ref{assp: private counts ).}
elements.sent-2411-473: This demands a careful construction that is robust to statistical noise, allowing the algorithm to focus its learning on the core, reachable parts of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-474: This principle is a core departure from prior work, designed to manage the dual trade-offs of exploration vs.~exploitation and privacy vs.~regret.
elements.sent-2411-475: Exploration vs.~exploitation: The schedule of increasing batch sizes manages this trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-476: Early, smaller batches prioritize exploration by enabling frequent policy updates to quickly move away from a poor initial policy.
elements.sent-2411-477: As the algorithm's confidence grows, larger batches are used for longer periods of confident exploitation, leveraging higher-quality data to reliably eliminate sub-optimal policies.
elements.sent-2411-478: Regret control: Crucially, the exponential growth rate (i.e., $L_b \propto 2^b$) is mathematically essential.
elements.sent-2411-479: Our analysis shows that this specific schedule ensures two things simultaneously: the non-private regret term matches the lower bound in standard episodic RL, while the privacy cost in regret becomes a logarithmic and additive term.
elements.sent-2411-480: Any other schedule would result in a suboptimal privacy cost.
elements.sent-2411-481: Forgetting: This batching strategy is paired with a ``forgetting" mechanism, using only data from the current stage for estimates, to prevent the accumulation of noise from previous, less accurate stages.
elements.sent-2411-482: A third innovation of SDP-PE lies in policy design for the fine exploration phase.
elements.sent-2411-483: Unlike its counterpart in APEVE, our policy is specifically designed to guarantee uniform data coverage over all active policies even in the presence of privacy noise.
elements.sent-2411-484: This is critical for deriving the tighter confidence bounds needed to achieve our final regret.
elements.sent-2411-485: The following sections will now elaborate on the components of this framework, detailing the private counting mechanisms and each of the three procedural steps.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{./figs/SDP-PE-0811.pdf} \caption{A visualization of the procedures of SDP-PE (Algorithm~\ref{algo: Private Batch-based Policy Elimination}).} \label{fig: SDP-PE}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: { \caption{Shuffle DP Policy Elimination (SDP-PE)} Parameters: Episode number $\episodetotal$, universal constant $C$, failure probability $\delta$, privacy budget $\pripara > 0$ and a Privatizer. \newline Initialization: \sloppy $\policyset_1 = \{\text{all deterministic policies}\}$, $\iota = \log(2\horizontotal\actionsize\episodetotal/\delta)$. \text{Set precision levels $\confcountxa$ for Privatizer.} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$\batchindx=1$ to $\batchnumber$} \IF{$2(\sum_{i=1}^\batchindx \batchlength_i)\geq\episodetotal$} \STATE $\batchlength_\batchindx = \frac{\episodetotal - 2(\sum_{i=1}^\batchindx \batchlength_i)}{2}$. (o.w. $\batchlength_\batchindx = 2^\batchindx$) \ENDIF \STATE $\infreqtuples^\batchindx,\!\crutrans,\!\policy_\batchindx\! =\! \text{CrudeExploration}(\policyset_\batchindx,\batchlength_\batchindx,\pripara,\!\text{Privatizer}).$ \STATE $\reftrans,\rewprieasy^\batchindx = \text{FineExploration}(\infreqtuples^\batchindx,\crutrans,\policyset_\batchindx, \policy_\batchindx, \batchlength_\batchindx,\pripara, $ $\text{Privatizer})$ \STATE $\elimpolicyset_\batchindx = \emptyset$ \FOR{$\policy \in \policyset_\batchindx$} \IF{$\sup_{\mu\in\policyset_\batchindx}\valuef^{\mu}(\rewprieasy^\batchindx,\reftrans) - \valuef^\policy(\rewprieasy^\batchindx,\reftrans) \geq 2C\big(\sqrt{\frac{\statesize\actionsize\horizontotal^3\iota}{\batchlength_\batchindx}}+\frac{\statesize^3\actionsize\horizontotal^5\confcountxa\iota}{\batchlength_\batchindx}\big)$} \STATE Update $\elimpolicyset_\batchindx\leftarrow \elimpolicyset_\batchindx \cup \{\policy\}$. \ENDIF \ENDFOR \STATE $\policyset_{\batchindx+1} \leftarrow \policyset_{\batchindx} \backslash \elimpolicyset_\batchindx$. \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic} \label{algo: Private Batch-based Policy Elimination}}
elements.subsection-2411-021: Model Estimation via Private Counts in Algorithm \ref{algo: Private Batch-based Policy Elimination}
elements.sent-2411-486: SDP-PE employs a model-based approach, relying on a ``forgetting'' principle to estimate the MDP model in each stage.
elements.sent-2411-487: This means that only the data from the current batch is used for model estimation.
elements.sent-2411-488: While the specific dataset construction varies between the crude exploration and fine exploration steps, as will be detailed later, the general process for estimating the model from a batch trajectories is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-489: Given a batch dataset $\batchdata$ from $n$ users, we define the true visitation counts and cumulative rewards at each step $\horizon$, $\visitxaxtotaleasy_\horizon(\state,\action,\state')\!:=\!\sum_{i=1 ^{n \ind\{\state_\horizon^{i ,\action_\horizon^{i ,\state_{\horizon+1 ^{i = \state,\action,\state'\}$, similarly $\visitxatotaleasy_\horizon(\state,\action)\!\!:=\!\!\sum_{\state^\prime\in\statespace \visitxaxtotaleasy_\horizon(\state,\action,\state')$, and $\rewcumeasy_\horizon(\state,\action)\!:=\!\sum_{i=1 ^{n \ind\{\state_\horizon^{i ,\action_\horizon^{i = \state,\action\} \cdot \reward_\horizon^{i}$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-490: These empirical counts are then processed by a shuffle Privatizer, which then releases their privatized versions, denoted as $\visitxatotalprieasy_\horizon(\state,\action,\state'),\visitxaxtotalprieasy_\horizon(\state,\action)$, and $\rewcumprieasy_\horizon(\state,\action)$.
elements.sent-2411-491: As formalized in Assumption~\ref{assp: private counts , these private counts are guaranteed to closely approximate the true value, which is essential for reliable learning.}
elements.sent-2411-492: The detailed implementation of such Privatizer is justified in Section~\ref{sec: Privacy Guarantees .}
elements.sent-2411-493: A key property of our Privatizer, enforced by Assumption \ref{assp: private counts , is that it maintains summation consistency, i.e., $\visitxatotalprieasy_\horizon(\state,\action) = \sum_{\state^\prime\in\statespace \visitxaxtotalprieasy_\horizon(\state,\action,\state')$.}}

elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{Crude Exploration} Input: Policy set $\policyset$, number of episodes $\batchlength$, privacy budget $\pripara$ and a Privatizer. Initialization: $\batchlength_0 = \frac{\batchlength}{\horizontotal}, C_1=6, \infreqtuples=\emptyset,\iota = \log(2\horizontotal\actionsize\episodetotal/\delta)$. $1_{\horizon,\state,\action}$ is a reward function $\reward'$ where $\reward'_{\horizon'}(\state',\action') = \ind\{(\horizon',\state',\action')=(\horizon,\state,\action)\}$. $\absorbstate$ is an additional absorbing state. $\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}}$ is a transition function over extended space $\statespace\cup\{\absorbstate\}\times\actionspace$, initialized arbitrarily. C_1\confcountxa\horizontotal^2\iota\}\!.$ \STATE $\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}} = \text{Estimate Transition}(\visitxatotalprieasy^{\text{cru}}_\horizon,\infreqtuples,\absorbstate,\horizon,\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}}).$ \ENDFOR \STATE Policy $\policy_0 \leftarrow$ uniform mixture of $\{\policy^{\text{cru}}_{\horizon,\state,\action}\}_{(\horizon,\state,\action)}$. \RETURN $\{\infreqtuples,\tilde{P}^{\text{cru}},\policy_0\}$. \end{algorithmic} \label{algo: Crude Exploration}
elements.sent-2411-495: The first step, crude exploration (Algorithm \ref{algo: Crude Exploration ), is designed to build a preliminary, or ``crude," model of the environment $\crutrans_\horizon(\state'\vert\state,\action)$ for any tuple $(\horizon,\state,\action,\state')$.}
elements.sent-2411-496: The main challenge is that some state-action pairs may be too rarely visited to gather sufficient statistics, especially under privacy constraints.
elements.sent-2411-497: Our strategy is to formally partition the system's state space by identifying a set of infrequently-visited tuples, denoted as $\infreqtuples$.
elements.sent-2411-498: The key idea behind is that for the tuples not in $\infreqtuples$, we can get accurate estimates, and for tuples in $\infreqtuples$, they have little influence on the value estimate.
elements.sent-2411-499: To identify this set, we perform a layer-wise exploration.
elements.sent-2411-500: For each step $\horizon$ of episode, we explore every state-action pair $(\state,\action)$ by constructing a policy, $\policy^{\text{cru ,\batchindx _{\horizon,\state,\action}$, that maximizes the estimated probability of visiting that pair under the current model.}}
elements.sent-2411-501: We then form a single exploration policy for the layer $\policy^{\text{cru ,\batchindx _{\horizon}$, a uniform mixture of these specialized policies.}}
elements.sent-2411-502: This mixture policy is executed and the resulting trajectories $\batchdata^\horizon$ are batched and sent to the Privatizer.
elements.sent-2411-503: These returned private counts are then used to empirically identify all tuples visited fewer than $\cO(\confcountxa\horizontotal^2\iota)$ times, which form the set $\infreqtuples$.
elements.sent-2411-504: This set is then used to construct an absorbing MDP, a technique adapted from \cite{qiao2022sample .}
elements.sent-2411-505: As fomally defined below, this method isolates the hard-to-reach states by redirecting their transition probabilities to a single absorbing state, $\absorbstate$.
elements.sent-2411-507: The final output is $\crutrans$ derived from Equation~\eqref{eq: private transition estimate , a private and reliable crude estimate of the absorbing MDP.}

elements.sent-2411-602: In this paper, we introduced Shuffle Differentially Private Policy Elimination (SDP-PE), the first generic algorithm for reinforcement learning under the shuffle privacy model.
elements.sent-2411-603: Our method adapts the policy elimination paradigm to the private learning setting by introducing a novel exponential batching schedule paired with a "forgetting" mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-604: When instantiated with our proposed Privatizer, SDP-PE achieves a regret of $\widetilde{\cO}\rbr{\sqrt{\statesize\actionsize\horizontotal^3\episodetotal + \frac{\statesize^3\actionsize\horizontotal^6 {\pripara }$, matching the information-theoretic lower bound up to lower-order terms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-605: Notably, this result achieves utility comparable to the centralized (JDP) model but under a stronger privacy guarantee, while significantly outperforming the local (LDP) model.
elements.sent-2411-606: Our numerical experiments also corroborate these findings and demonstrate the practical advantages of our method.
elements.sent-2411-607: This work thus demonstrates the viability of the shuffle model for achieving a favorable privacy-regret trade-off in data-driven control for advanced CPS.
elements.sent-2411-608: This work opens several avenues for future research.
elements.sent-2411-609: Key directions include improving the computational efficiency of the algorithm for real-time CPS deployment, extending our theoretical guarantees to the setting of function approximation, and exploring the application of alternative privacy notions, such as Rényi DP.. .

elements.sent-2411-616: First, we show that our private crude model ($\crutrans$) is multiplicatively close to the absorbing model for all well-explored tuples.
elements.sent-2411-617: Second, we leverage this to show that for any active policy, the probability of visiting an "infrequently-visited" tuple is small.
elements.sent-2411-618: Finally, we use this low probability to bound the total difference in value.
